nativeRange.habitat: Shaded reef walls, cave interiors, and overhangs in areas of consistent strong water movement; typically found in areas with low ambient light and high particulate nutrient availability

decorPreferences: secured upright with aquarium-safe epoxy or putty
shaded placement or under overhangs to reduce microalgae colonization
open water column position with consistent high flow
middle zone placement with spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Fully azooxanthellate (non-photosynthetic); derives no energy from light and relies entirely on capturing planktonic food from the water column. Target feeding is generally considered mandatory for long-term survival. Suitable foods include phytoplankton, live or frozen baby brine shrimp nauplii, rotifers, copepods, Mysis shrimp, Marine Snow, oyster eggs, and commercial filter-feeder preparations. Daily feeding is commonly advised; reducing flow during feeding periods has been reported to improve food contact with polyps. Supplemental iodine, strontium, calcium, and trace elements are commonly recommended alongside regular water changes.

goodWith: seahorses (commonly paired in NPS species tanks)
avoidWith: anemones (contact tends to cause tissue damage)
aggressive corals placed in direct contact
urchins and large hermit crabs that may climb and damage branches

summary: Vivid Indo-Pacific NPS gorgonian with a red/orange skeleton and intense blue polyps. Fully azooxanthellate; expert-level care with mandatory daily target feeding and high non-laminar flow.
careNotes:
Taxonomic note: The trade name 'Blueberry Sea Fan' is applied to gorgonians of the genus Acalycigorgia (family Acanthogorgiidae). WoRMS currently treats Acalycigorgia as a junior synonym of Acanthogorgia Gray, 1857; the 'sp.' designation in the scientific name reflects that multiple species are sold under this trade name without definitive species-level identification at point of sale. The genus belongs to order Malacalcyonacea (formerly Alcyonacea) based on current classification.

The Blueberry Sea Fan is one of the most visually striking NPS corals available in the reef hobby, featuring vivid blue polyps contrasting against a red or orange gorgonian skeleton. It is generally considered suitable only for skilled, experienced reef aquarists due to its intensive feeding requirements and low tolerance for system instability.

Key care requirements:

Feeding: Daily target feeding is widely considered the single most critical factor for long-term survival. Suitable foods include phytoplankton, rotifers, copepods, baby brine shrimp nauplii, Mysis shrimp, Marine Snow, oyster eggs, and dedicated filter-feeder commercial preparations (e.g., Roti-Feast, Oyster Feast, TDO-A, Cyclop-Eeze). Reducing water flow during feeding periods tends to improve food contact. Supplemental dosing of iodine, strontium, calcium, and trace elements is commonly recommended alongside regular partial water changes.

Lighting: Low lighting is generally advised — the species does not require light and low-intensity conditions tend to suppress microalgae colonization of the skeleton and branches. Placement in shaded areas, under overhangs, or away from direct light fixtures is commonly recommended.

Flow: High, non-laminar (chaotic or surge-type) flow is generally considered essential. As a filter feeder the species depends on water movement to deliver particulate food to its polyps, and stagnant or low-flow pockets around the colony tend to promote microalgae and cyanobacteria growth that can rapidly degrade tissue.

Algae and cyanobacteria control: Colonization of branches by algae or cyanobacteria is the most commonly cited cause of decline after insufficient feeding. Maintaining vigorous flow around the colony and keeping nutrient levels in a balanced range (moderate nitrate 1–10 ppm, phosphate 0.01–0.05 ppm) has been reported to reduce this risk.

Mounting: The base should generally be anchored to live rock with aquarium-safe epoxy or two-part putty. Branches tend to be somewhat brittle — careful handling during placement is advisable.

System maturity: A well-established reef system (typically 6+ months old) with a diverse biological filtration base and a copepod/zooplankton population is generally considered prerequisite. Fish presence is often cited as beneficial for providing dissolved organics and additional planktonic food.

Water parameters: Temperature 23–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm. Multiple sources agree on this range; TankFacts extended temperature upper end to 80°F (26.7°C) while LiveAquaria cites 72–78°F (22–25.6°C); the working range averages across all three sources consulted.

Fragmentation (fragging) is the primary captive propagation method; branches can be cut and attached to frag plugs or rubble. Sexual reproduction is not practiced in the hobby.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation (fragging) of branches is the primary captive propagation method and is relatively straightforward compared to stony corals. Sexual reproduction occurs in the wild but is not commonly attempted in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Blueberry Sea Fan
primary.notes: Primary species page. Provided water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (Expert Only), lighting (low), feeding frequency (several times per week), target foods (micro-plankton, baby brine shrimp, filter-feeder foods), placement (middle to top), supplements (iodine, strontium, calcium, trace elements), and husbandry notes.

additional.name: TankFacts – Blueberry Sea Fan (Acalycigorgia sp.)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, dKH 8–12, Ca 380–450 ppm, Mg 1200–1400 ppm. Temperature upper bound 26.7°C averaged with LiveAquaria and LiveRockNReef to yield final working max of 27°C. Confirmed non-photosynthetic NPS status, high flow, low lighting, expert care level, and peaceful temperament.

additional.name: LiveRockNReef – Blueberry Gorgonian (Acalycigorgia sp.)
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (74–82°F / 23–28°C), SG (1.020–1.027), pH (7.9–8.5), placement (middle to top), flow (high), diet (carnivorous, micro-plankton, mysis, baby brine shrimp), and Indo-Pacific (Indonesia) native range. Temperature upper end wider than LiveAquaria; averaged across all three sources.

additional.name: Oceans Garden – Blueberry Sea Fan
additional.notes: Confirmed expert care level, adult size 6–12 inches (15–30 cm), low lighting, strong flow, middle to top placement, peaceful temperament, reef safe, NPS filter-feeder requiring supplemental feeding several times per week.

additional.name: WoRMS – Acalycigorgia inermis (taxonomy reference)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only. Confirms family Acanthogorgiidae, order Malacalcyonacea, and that Acalycigorgia is currently treated as a junior synonym of Acanthogorgia. Distribution includes South Korea, Japan, Madagascar, and adjacent Indo-Pacific/Indian Ocean waters. Not used for husbandry parameters.
